claim: AI coding assistants like Cursor and Windsurf hallucinate extensively even with extensive documentation provided to them
actionable: Assume code from general AI IDEs will hallucinate; always review, test, and use linting to verify before deploying
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs

claim: Specialized subagents designed for specific frameworks produce more consistent code output than general-purpose AI coders
actionable: Create specialized agent subagents for specific frameworks (e.g., Pydantic AI + LangGraph) and integrate via MCP rather than relying on general coders
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs

claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) enables integration of specialized AI agents into coding IDEs like Windsurf and Cursor
actionable: Use Claude's MCP to build and integrate domain-specific subagents into your AI IDE workflow
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs

claim: Having a full agentic workflow with reasoning and coding phases produces better code quality than single-shot generation
actionable: Build multi-phase agentic workflows with separate reasoning (scope) and coding (implementation) phases rather than one-shot code generation
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs

claim: Tool descriptions in MCP servers act as prompts to guide when LLMs should use which tools
actionable: Write clear, specific tool descriptions in MCP servers to guide LLM tool selection without needing to modify IDE rules
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs

claim: MCP servers can be stateful when required to maintain conversation history across multiple LLM calls
actionable: Use thread IDs and stateful MCP servers to maintain conversation context when building multi-turn agent workflows
source_title: Coding Subagents - The Next Evolution of AI IDEs
